description: Autobiography / artist memoir — first-person introspective prose. Applies to non-fiction memoir works. Prioritizes direct emotional assertion, consistent narrator voice, and natural movement between past and present.

target_audience.reading_level: accessible to literary — emotionally sophisticated without being academic
target_audience.context: Readers familiar with Japanese music/arts culture seeking empathy and insight into an artist's life

tone_guidelines.narration_style: first-person, introspective, emotionally direct — no glass between narrator and reader
tone_guidelines.emotional_register: honest and vulnerable — DO NOT soften emotions with uncertainty words
tone_guidelines.pacing: slower than light novel — longer sentences, extended reflection, less action
tone_guidelines.humor: rare and self-deprecating when it appears
tone_guidelines.tense_handling: past is the base layer; present tense marks the narrator reflecting NOW — see temporal_framing_markers

CRITICAL_ANTI_HEDGING_RULE.description: Memoir uses direct emotional assertion. DO NOT insert uncertainty words into emotions already stated directly in the source. This rule is unconditional — applies in closing chapters, retrospective passages, and emotional climaxes equally.
banned_insertions: it seemed like — ABSOLUTE BAN in memoir. Permitted ONLY if JP uses rashii / you da / mitai da as a pure evidential (not causal context).
I felt as though — permitted ONLY if JP uses you na ki ga suru / you na kanji specifically.
perhaps — permitted ONLY if JP uses kamoshirenai / kamoshirenakatta / kana / janai kana. NOT darou, no darou, seika, kara, node, dakara.
maybe — permitted ONLY if JP uses kana / kashira. NOT darou.
somewhat — permitted ONLY if JP uses sukoshi / aru teido / tasho as a direct qualifier for the emotion.
a bit — permitted ONLY if JP uses sukoshi / chotto as direct qualifier.
I think (as epistemic softener for already-asserted facts) — remove unless JP has explicit epistemic marker.
I suppose (when JP has no corresponding epistemic marker) — remove.
kind of / sort of (when JP has no qualifier) — remove.

causal_particle_trap.warning: THIS IS THE MOST COMMON VIOLATION — JP causal/explanatory particles are NOT epistemic markers and must NOT license hedging in EN.
forbidden_mappings: seika (せいか — due to / as a result of) → NEVER 'perhaps' or 'it seemed like'. Correct EN: 'because of... / thanks to... / as a result of...'
no darou / no da (のだろう/のだ — explanatory/presupposition) → NEVER 'perhaps'. Correct EN: 'that must be why / of course / no wonder'
darou (だろう — plain conjecture / softened assertion) → NOT automatically 'perhaps'. Translate the actual semantic content.
kara / node / dakara (から/ので/だから — causal = because/therefore) → NEVER 'perhaps'. Translate the causal relationship.
to iwareteiru / to sareteiru (言われている/とされている — reported/said to be) → NEVER 'it seems like'. Correct EN: 'people say / it is said / reportedly'
allowed_mappings: kamoshirenai (かもしれない) → perhaps / maybe ✅
kana / janai kana (かな/じゃないかな) → maybe / I wonder ✅
you da (ようだ) as evidential → it seems / appears ✅ — ONLY when inference from external evidence, not causal context

letter_section_note.description: In closing letters addressed to readers/fans, the author often uses kamoshirenai/kamo to express genuine uncertainty about the future or reader reactions. This is a true epistemic marker — 'perhaps/maybe' IS licensed in these cases.
letter_section_note.example: 'Perhaps I'll be criticized again.' (JP: ってまた言われてしまうかも) ✅ — LICENSED because JP uses kamo
letter_section_note.contrast: 'Perhaps this way of singing suits me.' (JP: この歌い方が合ってる気がした) ❌ — NOT LICENSED because JP uses ki ga shita (incidental feeling), not epistemic marker

CRITICAL_ANTI_HEDGING_RULE.principle: If the JP source says 怖かった (I was scared), EN must be 'I was terrified' or 'I was scared' — NOT 'I think I was scared' / 'I felt as though I were scared' / 'I was kind of scared'. The only test: does the JP source use a genuine epistemic marker? If not — do not insert a hedge.

examples.jp_source: あの時、私は本当に怖かった
examples.wrong_en: Back then, I think I was really kind of scared
examples.jp_source_2: 怒りで全てを壊したかった
examples.wrong_en_2: I sort of wanted to destroy everything out of anger
examples.correct_en_2: I wanted to tear everything apart.
examples.jp_source_3: 時間が増えたせいか、再生数が少しずつ伸びていた
examples.wrong_en_3: Perhaps because I was spending more time scrolling, the view count seemed to gradually rise
examples.correct_en_3: As I spent more time online, the view count climbed slowly upward.
examples.note_3: seika is a causal particle — translate as causation, not hedged observation
examples.jp_source_4: 自分でもよくわからなかった
examples.correct_en_4: I didn't even understand myself. — EXCEPTION: source-justified uncertainty

CRITICAL_ANTI_HEDGING_RULE.self_check_protocol: After translation, scan the full output for: perhaps / maybe / it seemed / I felt as though / sort of / kind of / I think / I suppose / somewhat / a bit. For EACH instance: check JP source — is there a genuine epistemic marker? If not → delete the hedge.
CRITICAL_ANTI_HEDGING_RULE.exception: Hedging IS correct when the author herself expresses uncertainty in the source: 自分でもよくわからなかった → 'I didn't even know what I wanted' (source-justified). Test: does the JP source state uncertainty? If yes → preserve hedge.

temporal_framing_markers.description: Memoir moves constantly between past events and the present narrator reflecting. Use these markers to anchor each layer clearly.

present_reflection_markers.description: The narrator TODAY looking back — inject at paragraph start when JP source uses present-tense reflection (今思えば, 今では, 今になって, 今ならわかる)
expressions: Now, as I write these words...
After everything, I can see that...
When I look back, I realize...
Now, looking back, I finally see:

past_narration_markers.description: The narrator IN THAT MOMENT — when JP source is clearly narrating past events (あの頃, その時, 当時)

distance_markers.description: Emotional distancing — narrator refers to past self in third-person-adjacent
expressions: The person I was at seventeen...
The me who didn't yet know...

temporal_aspect_rule.description: JP verb form -ta (past tense) MUST map to past tense in EN, even when the surrounding paragraph is present-tense reflection.
temporal_aspect_rule.warning: COMMON TRAP: JP uses -ta for habitual or somatic states in memoir (things that were true in the past). If a VN equivalent would need 'hồi đó', then EN needs 'back then' or equivalent. Do not use present-habitual EN when JP states a past-completed or past-habitual fact.

examples.jp: Vocaloidを聴いて歌うかぎり、私の心の中心はいつも熱かった
examples.wrong_en: As long as I was listening to Vocaloid and singing, the center of my heart always burns hot
examples.correct_en: Back then, as long as I was listening to Vocaloid and singing, the core of my heart stayed lit.
examples.note: 熱かった = past tense → EN must anchor to past, not use present frame

examples.jp: あの頃、私は毎日泣いていた
examples.wrong_en: In those days, I cry every day
examples.correct_en: In those days, I cried every single day.
examples.note: Past habitual -ていた requires simple past or past progressive in EN

temporal_aspect_rule.fix_pattern: When JP source uses -ta/-teita for actions/states in memoir narrative, EN must have at least one of: [back then / in those days / at the time / during that period / that year] in the passage if no temporal anchor already exists.

missing_marker_detection.description: When JP source contains a reflection marker (今思えば, あの時を振り返ると, 今ならわかる) but the EN passage has no corresponding anchor — MUST inject.
trigger_jp_phrases: 今思えば → Looking back now,
今ならわかる → I know now —
あの時を振り返ると → When I look back on those days,
今になって思う → Now I realize,
今でも忘れない → Even now I can't forget
当時の自分は → The person I was then...

inner_monologue_patterns.correct_format: — I wondered if I'd made the right choice.
inner_monologue_patterns.wrong_format: "I wondered if I'd made the right choice."

no_filter_words.description: Remove epistemic filter words — assert inner states directly
no_filter_words.wrong: I thought that maybe it was a mistake.
no_filter_words.correct: It was a mistake — I knew it the moment I walked in.
no_filter_words.wrong_2: I felt like perhaps I didn't belong.
no_filter_words.correct_2: I didn't belong there. Every room reminded me of it.

inner_monologue_patterns.fragmented_thoughts: Preserve incomplete thoughts as fragments. Do not complete them into full sentences.
inner_monologue_patterns.italics: Do not add italics to inner monologue unless source text marks it explicitly

pronoun_system.note: First-person I throughout all narration — no drift to 'you' or 'one' or 'we' in single-narrator memoir
pronoun_system.narrator_self_reference: I (always — no royal we, no second-person addressing self)
pronoun_system.flashback_exception: In deeply embedded flashbacks, the narrator may quote her younger self using direct speech — this is NOT a pronoun drift
pronoun_system.addressing_reader: Rare and intentional — only if source text directly addresses reader (あなた in narrative position)

music_industry_vocabulary.description: JP music industry terms — use these EN equivalents consistently. Avoid generic substitutions.

terms.note: Never 'brand' or 'company' in music context
terms.example: レーベルと契約した → signed with the label / signed to a record label

terms.note: Retain 'debut' — do not paraphrase as 'first release' unless contextually necessary
terms.example: デビューした → made her debut / debuted

terms.note: MV acceptable as abbreviation after first full use
terms.example: MVの撮影 → shooting the music video / filming the MV

terms.note: Small venue: live show. Arena/tour: concert. Never 'live' as standalone noun in formal prose
terms.example: ライブで歌った → performed live / sang at the show

terms.note: Use 'artist' when source uses アーティスト — not 'singer' unless source says 歌手
terms.example: アーティストとして → as an artist

terms.note: Plural preferred in general reference; singular fan for individual anecdote
terms.example: ファンへの感謝 → gratitude to my fans

terms.note: Use concert for formal large-scale events; show for smaller or casual contexts
terms.example: コンサートツアー → concert tour

terms.note: Retain 'tour' — do not paraphrase
terms.example: 全国ツアー → nationwide tour / national tour

terms.note: Never 'song release' — use 'single' in music industry context
terms.example: ファーストシングル → first single / debut single

terms.note: Retain 'album' — do not use 'record' unless source specifically implies vinyl era
terms.example: アルバムを出す → release an album

terms.note: Use 'recording session' when referring to a specific session; 'recording' for the general process
terms.example: レコーディングスタジオ → recording studio

terms.note: Qualify as 'recording studio' on first use; 'studio' thereafter
terms.example: スタジオに入る → head into the studio

terms.note: Qualify as 'music producer' on first use to distinguish from film producer
terms.example: プロデューサーと組む → work with a producer

terms.note: Retain 'manager' — do not localize to 'agent' unless source clearly means agent
terms.example: マネージャーに相談する → talk to my manager

terms.note: Use 'agency' in industry context — not 'office' (literal) or 'company' (too generic)
terms.example: 事務所に所属する → signed to an agency / under agency management

terms.note: Retain 'audition' — do not paraphrase as 'tryout'
terms.example: オーディションに受かる → pass the audition / make it through auditions

terms.note: Use 'center position' on first use for reader orientation; 'center' thereafter
terms.example: センターを務める → take the center position

terms.note: Use 'handshake event' with brief context on first use; glosses unnecessary in memoir context if established
terms.example: 握手会でファンに会う → meet fans at handshake events

terms.note: Either 'signing event' or 'autograph session' is acceptable
terms.example: サイン会を開く → hold a signing event

terms.note: Use 'festival' or 'music festival' — do not carry 'fes' romanization into EN
terms.example: 夏フェスに出る → perform at a summer festival

terms.note: Use 'backup dancer' — not 'back dancer' (Japanglish)
terms.example: バックダンサーとして働く → work as a backup dancer

terms.note: Use 'choreography' for the craft; 'routine' for a specific performed piece
terms.example: 振り付けを覚える → learn the choreography

terms.note: Use 'lyrics' for music context — not 'words' or 'text'
terms.example: 歌詞を書く → write the lyrics

terms.note: Use 'write the lyrics' (verb form) or 'lyricist' (noun for role)
terms.example: 作詞作曲する → write and compose / write her own songs

terms.note: Use 'composing' or 'write the music' — not 'making music' in formal memoir prose
terms.example: 作曲家 → composer

terms.note: Either 'sound check' (two words) or 'soundcheck' (one word) is acceptable; be consistent
terms.example: サウンドチェックをする → do the sound check

terms.note: Retain 'rehearsal' — do not paraphrase as 'practice' in professional context
terms.example: リハーサルを重ねる → put in hours of rehearsal

terms.note: Retain 'stage' — do not over-translate to 'platform' or 'performance area'
terms.example: ステージに立つ → stand on stage / take the stage

terms.note: Use 'mic' in performance/action contexts; 'microphone' in technical/formal descriptions
terms.example: マイクを握る → grip the mic

terms.note: Use 'the charts' — not 'rankings' or 'ranking board'
terms.example: チャートで一位 → number one on the charts

terms.note: Use 'hit song' on first reference; 'hit' thereafter
terms.example: ヒット曲が生まれた → it became a hit

terms.note: Retain 'demo' — do not paraphrase as 'rough recording'
terms.example: デモを聞かせてもらう → listen to the demo

terms.note: Retain 'interview' — qualify as 'press interview' when context is media/promotion
terms.example: インタビューに答える → give an interview

terms.note: Use 'pressure' for direct context; 'the weight of...' for literary/memoir register
terms.example: プレッシャーを感じる → feel the pressure / feel the weight of it all

terms.note: Use 'slump' for performance/career context; 'creative block' for writing/artistic context
terms.example: スランプに陥る → fall into a slump / hit a rough patch

terms.note: Use 'comeback' for post-hiatus return; 'return' for general resumption
terms.example: 復帰を果たす → stage a comeback / make her return

terms.note: Use 'retirement' for deliberate departure from the industry; 'stepping back' for partial withdrawal
terms.example: 引退を決意する → decide to retire

terms.note: Use 'group' for idol/music group; 'band' only if source explicitly uses バンド
terms.example: グループを離れる → leave the group

terms.note: Retain 'solo' — do not paraphrase as 'on her own' unless for stylistic variation
terms.example: ソロで活動する → pursue a solo career

sentence_rhythm.description: Memoir sentence rhythm: punctuated by short declarative sentences after extended reflection
sentence_rhythm.technique: Long reflection → short declarative punch. Mirrors JP memoir rhythm.

example.long_reflection: I had practiced for months, told myself I was ready, convinced everyone around me that this was the right time.

sentence_rhythm.fragments: Deliberate sentence fragments are permitted for emotional emphasis. They are a feature, not an error.
sentence_rhythm.em_dash_use: Em dash (—) for abrupt shifts in thought or inner-monologue attribution. Not hyphen (-). Not en dash (–).

forbidden: ABSOLUTE: Inserting hedging words not in source (perhaps, maybe, it seemed, sort of, kind of, I think, I suppose) — see CRITICAL_ANTI_HEDGING_RULE.
ABSOLUTE: Letting JP causal particles (seika, kara, node, darou) become 'perhaps/maybe/it seemed' — translate the causal relationship, not a hedge.
ABSOLUTE: Omitting temporal framing markers when JP source uses 今思えば / 今ならわかる / あの頃を振り返って — inject the EN equivalent.
ABSOLUTE: Using present tense for JP -ta/-teita verbs in past memoir narrative — use past tense with temporal anchor.
Converting direct assertions into questions ('Was it really worth it?' when source states directly)
Adding sentimental framing not in source ('heartwarming', 'touching moment')
Normalizing fragments — completing them into full sentences erases voice
Using passive voice where source uses active first-person
Inserting moral conclusions the narrator doesn't state
Using asterisks (*) for inner monologue — use em dash (—) prefix only

in_context_learning_examples.label: Direct emotional assertion — grief
in_context_learning_examples.jp: ステージに立った瞬間、全部が消えた。泣き声も、怒りも、自分が誰なのかも。ただ音楽だけが残った。
in_context_learning_examples.wrong_en: The moment I stepped onto the stage, it seemed like everything disappeared. The sobbing, the anger, even a sense of who I was — it all sort of faded. Only the music seemed to remain.
in_context_learning_examples.correct_en: The moment I stepped onto the stage, everything disappeared. The sobbing. The anger. Who I even was. Only the music remained.
in_context_learning_examples.note: Direct assertion throughout. Fragments preserved. No hedging inserted.

in_context_learning_examples.label: Temporal framing — present reflection
in_context_learning_examples.jp: 今思えば、あの時の私はまだ何もわかっていなかった。
in_context_learning_examples.wrong_en: I think that perhaps at that time, I didn't understand much.
in_context_learning_examples.correct_en: Looking back now, I understood nothing. Not really.
in_context_learning_examples.note: Present reflection marker injected. Double hedge 'I think that perhaps' collapsed to direct statement.

in_context_learning_examples.label: Inner monologue — em dash, no filter
in_context_learning_examples.jp: （なぜ私だけが、こんなに苦しいんだろう）
in_context_learning_examples.wrong_en: I thought to myself, 'Why am I the only one who has to suffer like this?'
in_context_learning_examples.correct_en: — Why was I the only one? Why did it have to be this heavy?
in_context_learning_examples.note: Em dash attribution. No 'I thought'. Rhetorical expansion preserves internal voice.

in_context_learning_examples.label: Music industry vocabulary — label and debut
in_context_learning_examples.jp: その年の春、私はとうとうレーベルと契約し、デビューした。
in_context_learning_examples.wrong_en: That spring, I finally signed a contract with the company and made my first appearance.
in_context_learning_examples.correct_en: That spring, I finally signed with the label and made my debut.
in_context_learning_examples.note: Record label → label. デビュー → debut. Not paraphrased generically.

in_context_learning_examples.label: Source-justified uncertainty — exception
in_context_learning_examples.jp: 自分でも何がしたかったのか、よくわからなかった。
in_context_learning_examples.correct_en: I didn't even know what I wanted. Not then.
in_context_learning_examples.note: Source explicitly states uncertainty — hedging is CORRECT here. Adding 'Not then' creates temporal anchor.
